front.title: SEcure access To Tls Local rEsources (SETTLE) Problem Statement and Requirements

abstract.t: This document defines the problem statement, common terminology, security models, and technical requirements for identifying, validating, and establishing secure connections with local network devices. It outlines the challenges of extending the Web's Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) to local, offline, or ad-hoc environments. The document specifies requirements for privacy-preserving discovery, generating and distributing local trust anchors, and establishing mutually authenticated secure contexts without relying on global certificate authorities or insecure Trust On First Use (TOFU) mechanisms.

section.title: Introduction
section.t: Unencrypted communications have historically been the default for servers on local networks such as printers, routers, and file-sharing servers. However, with the advent of HTTPS everywhere, browsers disadvantage unencrypted communications by requiring a Secure Context for features such as Service Workers, Web Bluetooth, WebCrypto, and WebTransport.

Furthermore, modern browser security models, such as Local Network Access (LNA), restrict public websites from making requests to private IP addresses without explicit secure contexts and user permissions, aiming to prevent "confused deputy" attacks against localhost and local devices. This significantly increases the importance of establishing a Secure Context for local domains. Non-HTTP protocols, such as SMB3 encrypted file sharing and printing (IPP over HTTPS), also benefit from stronger server authentication and encrypted communications.

Existing deployments of TLS on local networks typically use "self-signed" X.509 certificates that lack the endorsement of a trusted third party. Because the client User Agent lacks a cryptographic trust anchor for the local device, it presents a certificate warning. Users frequently disregard these warnings, leading to warning fatigue and leaving them vulnerable to active interception.

This document defines common terminology and requirements for discovering, identifying, validating, and establishing secure connections with local network devices without relying on Trust On First Use (TOFU) or continuous internet connectivity.

section.title: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

dl.dd: An environment meeting the minimum standards of authentication and confidentiality as defined by the W3C Secure Contexts specification .
The process of establishing an initial trust relationship and secure communication channel between a User Agent and a Device where no prior relationship exists.
An approach where a client blindly accepts the first cryptographic identity (e.g., public key or certificate) presented by a server without external verification.
An entity with a network interface that connects to a local network.
A Device used primarily by End Users to access services (e.g., a web browser on a smartphone).
A Device providing a service on the local network (e.g., printer, router, IoT hub).
A DNS or mDNS label for a Device. It MUST be locally unique, and SHOULD be globally unique to prevent collisions.

A mechanism for transferring trust entropy between a Device and a UA via a channel physically separate from the primary network interface. Different mechanisms offer varying security properties, including QR codes (visual, high entropy), PIN entry (manual, low entropy), Short Authentication Strings (SAS), Near Field Communication (NFC), or Push-Button Configuration (PBC).
A cryptographic public key or certificate that a UA implicitly trusts to verify the identity of a Target.
A specific Infrastructure Device designated to act as a local certificate authority or trust broker, transitioning a mesh of unverified devices into a hub-and-spoke trust topology.

section.title: Problem Statement and Formal Derivation
section.t: The core challenge of securing local network communications stems from the disconnect between the global Web PKI and local network topologies. Securing a connection requires clearing three hurdles:

ol.li: Resolution: The identifier provided by the user or discovered on the network must translate to an IP address.
Identity Binding: The certificate presented by the target must correspond to the requested name.
Trust Anchor Validation: The certificate must be cryptographically signed by a trust anchor recognized by the client.

section.t: On the public internet, these are solved via global DNS, Web PKI, and browser-bundled Root CAs. On a local network, devices reside behind NAT, use non-routable IP addresses , lack delegated public domain names, and may operate offline.

This represents an application of Zooko's Triangle : naming systems struggle to be simultaneously Human-meaningful, Secure, and Decentralized. To solve this, SETTLE must bypass the following flawed approaches:

ul.li: The Cloud/Public CA Workaround: Approaches using dynamic DNS to map local IPs to global subdomains (e.g., the "Plex model" using wildcards) require persistent internet connectivity, external infrastructure, and fail completely in air-gapped or offline environments.

Manufacturer-Only Trust: Relying solely on manufacturer-installed certificates (e.g., IDevID) creates a "naming disconnect" (the cert binds a key to a serial number, not a local name like "printer.local"). Furthermore, it excludes "self-hosted" software running on generic hardware.

Trust On First Use (TOFU): In a hostile local network (Active Attacker), accepting a self-signed certificate is cryptographically indistinguishable from accepting an attacker's key. It automates vulnerability rather than solving warning fatigue.

section.t: Conclusion: To establish Secure Contexts without Cloud or Manufacturer-only infrastructure, the system must utilize the User to mediate trust via an OOB Ceremony. Consequently, User Agents require a mechanism to ingest and store these user-verified roots in a dynamic local trust store (S_Local).

section.title: Prior Art and Related Work
section.t: The SETTLE architecture builds upon and differentiates itself from several existing approaches:

ul.li: BRSKI and BRSKI-PRM: Bootstrapping Remote Secure Key Infrastructure automates provisioning using manufacturer IDevIDs. While powerful for enterprise networks, it requires a supply chain root of trust, which is absent in self-hosted consumer scenarios.

Matter Protocol: Matter utilizes Password-Authenticated Session Establishment (PASE) for onboarding. However, security analyses have highlighted flaws including low-entropy (27-bit) passcodes and static salts, emphasizing the need for higher entropy in OOB ceremonies.

Wi-Fi Easy Connect (DPP): Uses QR codes containing public keys to bootstrap WPA3 credentials. This provides a strong precedent for OOB ceremonies, though it targets network-layer access rather than application-layer TLS identity.

AirDrop / PrivateDrop: Traditional local discovery using truncated hashes leaks user identifiers. Research into Private Set Intersection (PSI) demonstrates that privacy-preserving mutual discovery is feasible with sub-second latency on mobile devices.

SDSI/SPKI: Simple Distributed Security Infrastructure proposed a decentralized PKI binding authorizations directly to keys. While conceptually aligned with local trust, modern web browsers mandate X.509 for compatibility.

section.title: Operational Tracks
section.t: To prevent circular dependencies, SETTLE requirements are organized into two composable tracks. Implementers may choose the track appropriate for their ecosystem.

section.title: Track A: Direct Peer Bootstrapping
section.t: This track addresses the bootstrapping of a single link between a UA and a Target when shared trust is initially zero. It relies on an Out-of-Band (OOB) Ceremony (e.g., scanning a QR code) to transfer trust entropy and establish a mutual Trust Anchor directly. This track is optimized for ad-hoc connections and generic hardware, bridging the gap without relying on any intermediaries.

section.title: Track B: Scaled Trust via Referee
section.t: To avoid the user friction of performing manual OOB Ceremonies for every device, this track introduces a trusted local intermediary (a Referee or Local Manager). The user bootstraps trust with the Referee (via Track A). The Referee subsequently acts as a local Certificate Authority (e.g., using ACME or EST ) to issue credentials to other Infrastructure Devices. The UA inherently trusts the network via its binding to the Referee.

dl.dd: The system MUST provide a mechanism to assign unique names to local devices. To prevent collisions, names SHOULD incorporate high-entropy device-specific data.

Local device names MUST utilize standardized namespaces reserved for local or non-DNS use to ensure broad client support. Valid namespaces include ".local" for mDNS , "home.arpa." for unicast DNS , or ".alt" .
Name resolution MUST NOT depend on the availability of global DNS infrastructure.
The system MUST utilize standard local discovery protocols, such as DNS-Based Service Discovery (DNS-SD) , to locate devices prior to authentication.

The discovery mechanism MUST prevent passive tracking of devices or users. The system MUST NOT broadcast static identifiers (like MAC addresses or static account hashes) in plaintext if the device is portable. Systems SHOULD utilize rotating identifiers or cryptographic mechanisms like Private Set Intersection (PSI).
The architecture MUST NOT rely on Trust On First Use (TOFU) executed exclusively over the local network interface for initial authentication.

The system MUST define or profile an OOB mechanism to transfer trust entropy. The OOB material MUST provide sufficient entropy (e.g., at least 128 bits) to resist offline dictionary and GPU brute-force attacks.

The system SHOULD support the use of manufacturer-installed certificates (e.g., IDevID). When used, the system MUST define a mechanism to cryptographically bind the manufacturer-provided identity to the user-facing Local Device Name. This mechanism MUST NOT be the only method supported.

User Agents SHOULD implement a scoped, dynamic trust store capable of ingesting and persisting Trust Anchors generated or verified via OOB Ceremonies, independent of the OS-level global PKI trust store.
The bootstrapping protocol MUST support mutual authentication, allowing both the User Agent to verify the Target, and the Target to verify the authorization of the User Agent connecting to it.
All communications requiring a secure context MUST utilize TLS 1.3 or equivalent modern transport security protocols.
To minimize downgrade attacks, the system SHOULD utilize Implicit TLS (connect-then-handshake) rather than connection-upgrade mechanisms (e.g., STARTTLS) .
Device certificates MUST populate the Subject Alternative Name (SAN) extension. User Agents MUST verify these identities in accordance with .

The protocol MUST integrate seamlessly with browser Local Network Access (LNA) policies. Devices MUST be capable of serving verifiable certificates to answer preflights, enabling browsers to bypass user-hostile permission prompts.
The protocols chosen MUST support cryptographic agility to accommodate future algorithm deprecations and ensure interoperability between independent vendor implementations.
The system MUST operate securely without requiring Internet connectivity.
The system MUST support a hierarchical local trust model (Track B) where a mutually authenticated Referee can issue credentials to other local devices.
dl.dt: REQ-ARCH-03: Device Lifecycle and Key Rotation
dl.dd: The system MUST provide mechanisms for routine key rotation, certificate renewal without user intervention, and trust revocation. A factory reset of a Device MUST cryptographically invalidate and purge all prior local trust bindings.

The system MUST provide mechanisms to explicitly authorize clients for specific roles. Devices SHOULD provide a standard API allowing authorized controllers to audit all active trust bindings and ACLs to prevent "hidden" administrative fabrics.
User Agents and Referees MUST clearly present trust decisions and access control lists to the user, ensuring the user is actively aware of which devices are trusted.

section.title: Security Considerations
section.t: The SETTLE architecture assumes a hostile local network. The following threats must be mitigated by compliant implementations.

section.title: Evil Twin and Network Impersonation
section.t: An attacker may clone a local Wi-Fi access point (Evil Twin), spoof BSSIDs, or poison mDNS caches to redirect traffic to a malicious endpoint. Because SETTLE mandates an OOB Ceremony (REQ-TRUST-02) and rejects pure TOFU, the UA will detect the certificate mismatch and abort the connection, neutralizing the impersonation.

section.title: Social Engineering and Support Scams
section.t: If installing trust anchors is made significantly easier, malicious actors (e.g., fake "customer support") may instruct users to scan a malicious QR code, thereby installing a rogue trust anchor. UAs MUST provide clear, unambiguous UI warnings during the OOB ceremony about the implications of trusting a new device or Referee.

section.title: Confused Deputy Attacks
section.t: Public websites often attempt to use the browser as a "confused deputy" to attack local network devices via Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF). SETTLE's requirement for mutual authentication (REQ-TRUST-05) and compatibility with Local Network Access (LNA) policies (REQ-TRANS-04) ensures devices reject unauthenticated cross-origin requests.

section.title: Factory Reset Incompleteness
section.t: Research into IoT protocols has shown that hardware factory resets frequently fail to clear all administrative fabrics or ACLs, leaving devices vulnerable to prior owners. REQ-ARCH-03 mandates that a factory reset MUST completely destroy all cryptographic material linking the device to prior trust domains.

section.title: Supply Chain Attacks
section.t: When utilizing Pre-Installed Trust (IDevID), a compromised manufacturer or stolen batch of private keys allows an attacker to masquerade as a legitimate device. UAs should support mechanisms to constrain manufacturer trust to specific scopes or require secondary local validation.

section.title: Denial of Service
section.t: Local networks are vulnerable to resource exhaustion. Malicious peers may flood the network with mDNS advertisements, initiate thousands of TLS handshakes, or attempt to exhaust certificate enrollment limits (e.g., attacking a local ACME server). Implementations SHOULD employ rate-limiting and prioritize OOB-authenticated traffic.

section.title: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.

front.title: Names: Decentralized, Secure, Human-Meaningful: Choose Two
